input_text:
Launched from the Cape Canaveral site, Voyager 1 began its mission on September 5, 1977. Voyager 2 followed sixteen days later because NASA had established distinct objectives for the two probes. The journey, propelled by a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, had a primary objective spanning five years: to examine the outer planets, specifically Jupiter and Saturn, during a rare astronomical alignment of these enormous bodies, an occurrence happening roughly every 176 years.

The probe arrived at Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this passage, it transmitted the initial high-resolution imagery of the planet's subtle ring system and documented the vigorous volcanism on its moon Io, marking the first observation of such activity originating outside of Earth. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 made its closest approach to Saturn, successfully recording crisp visuals of its rings and capturing detailed views of Titan. This flyby of Titan caused Voyager 1 to leave the plane of the solar system, thereby concluding its planetary tour while simultaneously commencing its voyage into interstellar space.

Guided by a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 oriented its instruments back toward Earth on February 14, 1990, successfully capturing an image of our planet from a distance approaching 6 billion kilometers. This photograph, visible as a single pixel against the Sun's diffused brightness, gained lasting recognition as the Pale Blue Dot. This was the final photograph taken by the spacecraft; afterwards, the cameras were deactivated to conserve energy.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, achieving this feat as the first human-made object to do so. It carries the Golden Record, a gold-plated, 12-inch copper phonograph disc. This disc, developed with input including Sagan's, contains greetings in 55 languages, selected pieces of music, and visuals intended for any civilization that might eventually discover it. The spacecraft remains powered by three radioisotope thermoelectric generators utilizing plutonium-238, whose power source has been gradually diminishing due to its 87.7-year half-life.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is about 25 billion kilometers from Earth, maintaining a velocity near 61,000 kilometers per hour. Furthermore, in November 2026, it will reach the important milestone of having traveled one light-day.
output_text:
Launched from the Cape Canaveral location, Voyager 1 commenced its mission on September 5, 1977. Voyager 2 followed sixteen days after, as NASA had assigned separate goals to the two probes. The endeavor, launched aboard a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, had a core objective spanning five years: to observe the outer planets, specifically Jupiter and Saturn, during a rare astronomical alignment of these immense bodies, which occurs approximately every 176 years.

The probe arrived at Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this passage, it transmitted the initial high-resolution images of the planet's subtle ring system and documented the vigorous volcanism on its moon Io, marking the first recorded evidence of such activity originating outside of Earth. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 made its closest pass to Saturn, successfully recording clear visuals of its rings and obtaining detailed perspectives of Titan. This flyby of Titan caused Voyager 1 to depart the solar system's plane, thereby concluding its planetary tour while simultaneously beginning its journey into interstellar space.

Guided by a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 reoriented its instruments toward Earth on February 14, 1990, successfully capturing an image of our planet from a distance approaching 6 billion kilometers. This photograph, appearing as a solitary pixel against the Sun’s diffuse brightness, became enduringly known as the Pale Blue Dot. This was the last photograph taken by the spacecraft; thereafter, the cameras were shut down to conserve power.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, becoming the first human-made object to achieve this. It carries the Golden Record, a gold-plated, 12-inch copper phonograph disc. This disc, developed with input including Sagan's, contains greetings in 55 languages, selected musical pieces, and visuals intended for any civilization that might eventually find it. The spacecraft continues to be powered by three radioisotope thermoelectric generators using plutonium-238, whose power supply has been gradually waning due to its 87.7-year half-life.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated about 25 billion kilometers from Earth, maintaining a velocity near 61,000 kilometers per hour. Furthermore, in November 2026, it will reach the significant milestone of having traveled one light-day.
